coredata.dc:title: Dopamine sensing with fluorescence strategy based on low temperature co-fired ceramic technology modified with conducting polymers
coredata.dc:creator: Baluta, Sylwia
Malecha, Karol
Zając, Dorota
Sołoducho, Jadwiga
Cabaj, Joanna
coredata.dc:description: A convenient fluorescence sensing strategy for dopamine (DA) detection was developed based on polydopamine (poly(DA)) formed on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The prepared GQDs were highly luminescent due to the planar structure in aromatic molecules. The ceramic-based miniature biosensor was designed and constructed through the immobilization of laccase in an electrochemically synthesized polymer − poly[dithienotetraphenylsilane] based on low temperature co-fired ceramics technology (LTCC). This sensing system utilized the catalytic oxidation of DA to dopamine-o-quinone (DOQ), and then to poly(DA) (in alkaline conditions), which can selectively quench the strong luminescence of GQDs owing to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). The detection process was based on substarte oxidation in the presence of the enzyme − laccase. In optimized conditions, the analytical performance illustrated high sensitivity, selectivity in a broad linear range with detection limit of 80nM. Moreover, the method was successfully applied to test labeled pharmacological samples for DA.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence biosensor
Dopamine
LTCC
GQDs
Conducting polymer
Laccase
Microfluidic devices
FRET

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the sensor

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: LTCC layers in the construction of the microfluidic module.
LTCC layers in the construction of the holder for microfluidic module.
FT-IR spectrum of GQDs synthesized by pyrolysis of CA and CA.
Normalized emission spectrum of GQDs, λEx =365nm.
UV–vis absorption spectrum of GQDs and GQDs-poly(DA) mixture (various concentration of DA added during measurements).
Reduction in fluorescence of GQDs while increasing the concentration of DA in a solution (in microchannel of ceramic sensor), a − GQDs, b − GQDs and DA (50μM).
Exemplary AFM images and profiles of GQDs (A) and GQDs in mixture with DA − 50μM (B); 0.5×0.5μm.
Normalized fluorescence spectrum of GQDs in solution (λExc =365nm) − a/and absorption spectrum of poly(DA) solution.
Relationship of fluorescence intensity and dopamine detection with and without laccase in the setup.
Dependence between fluorescence intensity and a) pH of the test solution and b) mass of added citric acid.
A − The output signal of the flow-through biosensor; B– Fluorescence vs. different concentrations of the poly(DA) solutions; measurement conditions: flow rate −300μl/min, LED current: 10mA.
The output signal of the constructed biosensor for different flow rates (100μM/min–500μM/min).
The fluorescence intensities of GQDs with DA in the presence of 50μM of individual interfering substance and a mixture of all interferences. Concentration of dopamine=20μM.
Detection effect of DA based onp the method presented.

head.ce:title: Dopamine sensing with fluorescence strategy based on low temperature co-fired ceramic technology modified with conducting polymers

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Chemistry, Wrocław University of Science and Technology, Wybrzeże Wyspiańskiego 27, 50-370 Wrocław, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Wrocław University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Microsystem Electronics and Photonics, Wrocław University of Science and Technology, Wybrzeże Wyspiańskiego 27, 50-370 Wrocław, Poland

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Faculty of Microsystem Electronics and Photonics
Wrocław University of Science and Technology

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A convenient fluorescence sensing strategy for dopamine (DA) detection was developed based on polydopamine (poly(DA)) formed on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs). The prepared GQDs were highly luminescent due to the planar structure in aromatic molecules. The ceramic-based miniature biosensor was designed and constructed through the immobilization of laccase in an electrochemically synthesized polymer − poly[dithienotetraphenylsilane] based on low temperature co-fired ceramics technology (LTCC). This sensing system utilized the catalytic oxidation of DA to dopamine-o-quinone (DOQ), and then to poly(DA) (in alkaline conditions), which can selectively quench the strong luminescence of GQDs owing to Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). The detection process was based on substarte oxidation in the presence of the enzyme − laccase. In optimized conditions, the analytical performance illustrated high sensitivity, selectivity in a broad linear range with detection limit of 80nM. Moreover, the method was successfully applied to test labeled pharmacological samples for DA.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (3,4-dihydroxyphenyl ethylamine, DA), a catecholamine neurotransmitter, regulates many physiological processes in the central nervous, hormonal, renal and cardiovascular systems [1,2]. Changeable DA level in biological fluids (i.e. urine, blood plasma, and extracellular fluid of the central nervous system) can be a marker of several disorders such as schizophrenia and Huntington’s and Parkinson’s diseases [3]. Because of its significance in analytical techniques and diagnostics, sensitive and selective detection of DA is increasingly attracting a lot of attention in various areas of bio-analysis or biomedical research. DA concentration is currently monitored with such techniques as chromatography [4], spectroscopy [5], microdialysis techniques [6], electrochemical sensing [7], or colorimetric probes [1]. These methods, however, have some limitations. For instance chromatographic methods are time-consuming, labor intensive, and expensive. Similarly, the synthesis of fluorescent or colorimetric probes for dopamine sensing involves complicated procedures [8].

Recently, fluorescent techniques for detection of DA have attracted interests due to their reasonable cost, convenient control, and maneuverability in biological environments [9]. Although here seems to be a strong need for a sensitive and selective DA sensor, the development of a suitable method (for DA) is still limited [9].

A simple and quick alternative for dopamine detection is monitoring the oxidation product of dopamine [10] − a quinone derivative (of dopamine). The quinone (dopamine-o-quinone, DOQ) is (often) a product of DA oxidation in the presence of oxidoreductase (i.e. laccase) − Fig. 1 . This derivative, however, is unstable [11] and swiftly polymerized in alkaline conditions to polydopamine (poly(DA)), which is structurally similar to the biological eumelanin polymers [12].

A crucial factor in the construction of a biosensor is the need to achieve adequate and effective enzyme immobilization. The use of conducting polymers (CPs) for the construction of different biosensors has recently been studied extensively because of their redox, optical, mechanical and electrical properties. Conducting polymers are durable and stable, and are considered among the most suitable materials for biosensors [13]. Biosensor efficiency depends mainly on the surface architecture, interaction between the enzyme and electrode surface, and the protection of 3D structure of a biocatalyst. Therefore, CPs have turned out to be one of the most useful transducers because of their simple fabrication. CPs operate as a three-dimensional substrate for biomolecule deposition. Their charge transfer capacity acts as an excellent matrix for biomolecules providing enzyme mimetic environment [14].

In particular, the biosensor should be coated extensively with poly(3,4-ethylenedioxythiophene) (PEDOT) to improve its function and biocompatibility [15]. Luo group used PEDOT doped with carbon nanotubes (CNT) [16] and PEDOT doped graphene [17] coated GC electrodes for the detection of dopamine. Xue et al. modified the electrode with polyaniline composite to detect the serotonin in human serum [18].

Due to the presence of aromatic units in the polymer backbone, the immobilization was performed with the help of π–π stacking interactions of the polymer and enzymatic protein. These strong interactions stabilized the tertiary structure of proteins effectively [19]. Furthermore, sulfur-containing heteroatom materials were compared for their capacity to act as an immobilization matrix and for their redox mediator properties [20].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have demonstrated several attractive properties of standard carbon-based nanosized materials, such as low toxicity, biocompatibility, and photostability. In comparison with other carbon nanomaterials, GQDs exhibit a relatively high quantum yield [21]. Several studies on GQDs have recently focused on their properties, but research on the analytical adoption of this tool is still limited [9,22].

GQDs obtained in the process of pyrolysis of citric acid [23] have a passivated surface, and therefore, exhibit good fluorescence properties. The change in the surface of GQDs can cause FRET (Förster resonance energy transfer) and quench fluorescence intensity (of the designed biosensor). Despite the fact that some sensors proposed for DA detection are based on fluorescence strategy, the mechanism of DA detection still needsto be improved.
In this study, we report the fluorescence route for selective dopamine detection.

The detection system involved laccase immobilized in CP matrix (poly[dithienotetraphenylsilane]) and GQDs. The obtained quantum dots were used as fluorescent markers for quantitative detection of dopamine.

The designed sensor was made with LTCC (low temperature co-fired ceramics) technology [24]. The LTCC material is temperature and pressure resistant. A large majority of solvents, bases and moderately concentrated acids are inert to the fired LTCC material [25]. Moreover, various 3D structures (e.g. channels) can be made inside the LTCC-based substrate using laser, hot embossing or mechanical milling [26–28]. As a result, the LTCC-based substrate seems to be a convenient substrate for the construction of the lab-on-chip (LOC).

The presented LTCC-based biosensor for monitoring of dopamine consisted of a microfluidic chip and a chip holder with integrated optoelectronic components. The biosensor operated on the principle of measuring an efficient fluorescence quenching of GQDs. Thus the concentration of dopamine was monitored easily and inexpensively.

Laccase (from Cerrena unicolor, EC 1.10.3.2, ≥10U/mg) and all other reagents (dopamine hydrochloride, citric acid, tetrabutylammonium tetrafluoroborate − TBA-TFB) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade and were not purified further before use. The phosphate-citrate buffer solution (McIlvaine, pH 5.2) was prepared by mixing the citric acid and sodium phosphate solutions. The water used throughout the experiments was purified by Milipore system.
The silane derivative, dithienotetraphenylsilane (Fig. 2 ) was synthesized as in the previous experiment [29].

GQDs stock solution was prepared using 4g of pyrolyzed citric acid as described in the reported literature [23]. The pH (value) of quantum dots was adjusted to 10. The obtained GQDs were used as stock solution and stored at 4°C when not used. The size and homogeneity of the obtained GQDs were determined with AFM (Atomic Force Microscope) method in tapping mode (AFM NanoScope V Veeco). The measurements were performed at ambient air (25°C and relative humidity of 35%). The AFM used was the cutting edge TESP type, 10nm size and scanning rate of 3μm/s. The Fourier Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum was executed on Nicolet iS10 spectrometer. Emission spectrum of GQDs was measured using SILVER-Nova high performance spectrometer (StellarNet).

The fluorescence-based biosensor was constructed using LTCC technology (similar to the one presented in [24]). It was composed of 2 parts − a microfluidic module and a holder for the module − as presented in Fig. 3 .

The microfluidic module consisted of a microchannel for the analyte and two channels for optical fibres. The holder was composed of three cavities for LED (light emitting diode), photodetector and the microfluidic module. All components were made of 254μm-thick DP951 PX LTCC tape. The biosensor layout is presented in Fig. 4 (microfluidic module) and 5 (holder for the module). The microfluidic module consisted of 10 LTCC layers. The thickness of each layer was 254μm before firing. Two layers (Fig. 4a) defined the bottom of the module. An opening for the optical fibres (emission) was cut in these layers. A channel for the other optical fibres (excitation) and openings for fluidic ports were cut in two layers (Fig. 4b and c). A platinum electrode was deposited on the layer presented in Fig. 4c. The microfluidic channel, the channel for optical fibres and the opening for electrode’s terminal were made in two layers that can be seen in Fig. 4d. The microfluidic channel size was designed as 0.5×0.5mm2. The microfluidic module was sealed with four layers (Fig. 4e and f). The holder for the module was fabricated of 9 LTCC layers. Two layers were used to define the bottom of the holder (Fig. 5 a). The conductive lines for the photodetector were deposited on the layer presented in Fig. 5b. The orifices for the photodetector and electrical terminals were cut in the next four layers (Fig. 5c). The orifices for the microfluidic module and light source were made in two LTCC layers (Fig. 5d).

The microchannels for the analyte, channels for optical fibres and cavities were cut in unfired LTCC material using laser system (LPKF Protolaser U, λmax =355nm). The platinum electrode made of DuPont 9141R paste was deposited on the LTCC substrate using automatic screen-printer (Aurel VS 1520A). Structured ceramic tapes were laminated using an isostatic press (5MPa, 70°C) and co-fired (atmospheric air, Tmax =850°C). The dimensions of the constructed holder and microfluidic module were 4×2cm2 and 1.9×1.1cm2, respectively. In the final step, two PMMA optical fibres were glued to the fired LTCC-based microfluidic module. The LED (Optosource 260019) with peak wavelength at λmax =370nm and spectral half-width equal to Δλ1/2 =12nm was used as the excitation light source. The emission was measured by light-to-digital converter (TCS 3414) attached to the optical fibres. The TCS 3414 photodetector has a maximum spectral responsivity at λmax =460nm with Δλ1/2 =35 nm. Light source and photodetector were mounted on the LTCC-based holder using standard soldering technique. More details about the fabrication of the presented LTCC optical biosensor can be found in [31].
The LTCC-Pt electrode was modified with a thin film of poly(dithienotetraphenylsilane) and laccase.

The electropolymerization of monomer was performed using a potentiostat/galvanostat AUTOLAB PGSTAT128N with GPES software in a three-electrode electrochemical cell (5 ml) equipped with a working LTCC platinum electrode, Ag/AgCl reference electrode, and a coiled platinum wire as the counter electrode in accordance with [30].

Before each experiment, the solution was deoxygenated with nitrogen for 20min. All the electrochemical experiments were performed at room temperature. In order to synthesize the polymeric layer onto the surface of the clean electrode, dithienotetraphenylsilane (1mM) was dissolved in an acetonitrile solution containing 0.1M tetrabutylammonium tetrafluoroborate (TBA-TFB). The electrodes were dipped in 8ml of the monomer solution. The polymer layer deposition was carried out through cyclic voltammetry. The electrode was scanned in a potential range of 0.0–1.6V vs. Ag/AgCl for 10 cycles, at a scan rate of 50mV/s. Then, the polymer layer was washed with acetonitrile. The polymerization process was carried out on Pt layer of LTCC electrode (in microchannels) in batch conditions.

Laccase enzyme was immobilized on the electrochemically polymerized film above by physical adsorption. The 0.1M phosphate-citrate buffer (pH 5.2) containing laccase (1.5mg/ml) was injected using a syringe into the LTCC microchannel also equipped with a polymer-modified Pt layer. The deposition lasted for 2h at room temperature in batch conditions. The prepared enzyme setups were washed with buffer (pH 5.2) to remove unbound proteins, and stored at 4°C.
The electrode modified in this manner was introduced to the constructed detection setup before measurements.

Dopamine solutions (in various concentrations: 1–300μM) were prepared by dissolving an appropriate amount of DA in 0.25M Tris–HCl (pH=10). The solution of GQDs (in ratio 9:1) was admixed to each DA samples and stirred at 60°C for 60min.

The measurement setup consisted of a peristaltic pump, DC power supply and a computer for data collection. A UV Light Emitting Diode and a light-to-digital converter were supplied by DC signal (max. LED current 10mA). The output signals were collected using a computer. The dopamine level was determined in the flow system. Each DA sample solution was pumped to the LTCC module, wherein a LED illuminated the sample flowing through the flow channel, causing the fluorescence of the specimens present in the solution. The fluorescent light was collected by the light-to-digital converter connected to ATmega8-based transducer. It transmitted digital signal to the computer using two wire interfaces (TWI). More details about the transducer can be found in [31].

AFM measurements were performed to determine the topographical properties of synthesized GQDs (Fig. 6 ). Dots of spherical shape were observed, the height of a single dot being around 2.5-4.0nm (average size), and therefore in the nanocrystal range. The nanostructures tended to be monodisperse and regular in shape.

FTIR spectroscopic analysis was adopted to supply data on the functional groups in the formation of GQDs from citric acid (CA), as shown in Fig. 7 . The peaks at about 1751, 1552, 1260cm−1 indicated the presence of carboxyl, CO, and CO groups. The peaks demonstrated an effective formation of GQDs from CA.
The prepared GQDs displayed an evident absorption peak at 365nm (Fig. 7) due to π-π* transition of CC in the UV–vis spectrum as found in [23].

The fluorescence emission spectrum presented in Fig. 8 shows that maximum emission wavelength is 400nm (when excited at 365nm). As presented, the emission wavelength of GQDs was stable with the excitation wavelength ranging from 325nm to 375nm, illustrating its excitation-independent fluorescent property.

The convenient immobilization of the biocatalyst on solid substrates is a key step in the investigation of the biosensing tool. The support used for laccase immobilization should not impair the enzyme functionality. Herein, a polymer based on dithienotetraphenylsilane was electrochemically synthesized in the presence of 0.1M TBA-TFB [30]. The electropolymer occurred in the conducting, oxidized state.

The direct electrical contact between redox enzyme and the surface of the electrode must not be established in view of the donor–acceptor separation distance controlling the electron transfer rates. The electrical contact can be improved by the application of, e.g.,. conductive polymers. Modification of Pt electrode with the CP before deposition of laccase induces a strong immobilization of protein in the solid support − stronger than the adsorption of the protein immediately on the bare electrodes [32].

The polymeric films obtained under these conditions were of constant approximate thickness of 500nm. The thickness of the polymer layer under given conditions was estimated by means of the tapping mode AFM. The measurements were performed before placing Pt electrode in the measurement setup.

The feasibility for the designed sensors for DA detection was confirmed by the obtained UV–vis spectra and fluorescent images for different concentrations of DA in the solution. Dopamine itself has no fluorescence properties. Dopamine is known to self-polymerize to polydopamine (poly(DA)) in alkaline conditions, which are closely monitored by adjusting the pH value.

This poly(DA) complex possesses fluorescence properties and can be used for optical measurements. This characteristics prompts the ability of DA to attach onto the surfaces of solids, including nanomaterials, to form a thin poly(DA) film. As a result, the excess of DA induces self-polymerization of DA to poly(DA), which is then spontaneously adsorbed on the surface of GQDs, inducing FRET and subsequently, fluorescence quenching [33].
As seen in Fig. 9 , absorption decreased with the increase in the concentration of DA, indicating that the presence of DA could reduce the absorption values.

Moreover, the dependence between DA concentration and reply signals was determined via the brightness of GQDs at various concentrations of DA under a UV LED (irradiated by a UV-light with 365nm, Fig. 10 ). The bright photoluminescence (PL) of GQDs is strong enough to be visible to the naked eye.

The results showed that the blue fluorescence of GQDs was gradually decreased with the rise in DA concentration in the solution. The results suggest that adding DA can effectively quench the blue fluorescence of GQDs, specifically permitting the precise detection of dopamine in a sample.

The addition of DA to the sensing system to quench the fluorescence of GQDs through the formation of poly(DA) on the surface of carbon dots was also confirmed by Atomic Force Microscopy, as shown in Fig. 11 . The topographical changes of the formed poly(DA)/GQDs were differentiated by AFM as shown in Fig. 11. In the absence of DA, GQDs' size was around 2.5nm. The addition of DA at concentration of 50μM caused a topographical change which was connected with polymerization of DA to poly(DA) on GQDs surface, because a 13nm increase in size of the molecules was observed.

In this FRET sensing system, the poly(DA) was employed as the fluorescence acceptor (quencher). Fig. 12 shows the absorption spectrum of poly(DA) and fluorescence emission of spectrum of carbon QDs, respectively. It can be observed that emission spectrum of GQDs could overlap with the absorption spectrum of poly(DA) partly, specifying the potential to create effective energy transfer.

Upon addition of dopamine to the solution of GQDs, DA which is oxidized to DOQ (Fig. 1), and then swiftly polymerized in alkaline environment to polydopamine (poly(DA)), poly(DA) can be adsorbed onto GQDs. As a result, the fluorescence of GQDs can be quenched by poly(DA) through FRET in 1min in the designed microflow ceramic setup (Fig. 12). The fluorescence intensity of poly(DA)-GQDs system is proportional to the concentration of poly(DA), and the quenching constant (Ksv ) was calculated using the Stern-Volmer equation:
(1)
F0/F = Ksv
×[Q]+1
Where F0 and F are the fluorescent intensity of GQDs in the absence and presence of quencher, respectively. [Q] is the concentration of the quencher.
The Stern-Volmer equation: F0/F =1.138[poly(DA)] (μM)+1 (R =0.988).

In accordance to the slope of linear plot the quenching constant was calculated: Ksv
=0.138×109 M−1.

The catalytic activity of the immobilized laccase and the accuracy of the microfluidic chip were verified using optical measurements. The studies were performed with dopamine as laccase assay substrate. As it can be observed in Fig. 13 , the detection system with immobilized laccase improved the sensitivity and selectivity of the biosensor and lowered the detection limit. The developed laccase-based biosensor allows the direct oxidation of dopamine and accurate determination of the concentration of neurotransmitter in the test sample.

The laccase-mediated reaction was carried out in substrate solutions. Since the poly(DA) quenching effect is strongly dependent on the pH of the solution, especially when the catalyst is immobilized [34], the sensor response was measured at different values of the pH in the range from 8 to 10.5. The dopamine had greater quenching effect in alkaline environment than in acidic conditions. The highest fluorescence efficiency was found at pH 10.0, suggesting that this pH was the most favorable environment for the research strategy (Fig. 14 a). It was the result of enhancing polymerization of dopamine to poly-dopamine and its adsorption on carbon nanoparticles at pH 10.0. Formation of poly(DA) nanoparticles requires alkaline conditions, in which DA can be oxidized and spontaneously self-polymerize [35]. In the oxidation process of DA, the dopamine quinone (DQ) is formed, which is then converted to leukodopaminechrome (LDC). Then LDC can be oxidized to amonochrome (AC), which converts into 5,6-dihydroxyindole (DHI) − the monomer of polydopamine [36]. At this stage a proton is released, which causes a decrease in pH value. The intensity of fluorescence is also weaker [37].

The amount of pyrolyzed citric acid needed for the preparation of the optimal GQDs stock solution was also checked. As can be seen in Fig. 14b, GQDs demonstrated the best fluorescence intensity when 4g of citric acid was used.

To evaluate the linear range of the designed setup, its response to various concentrations of DA was measured. The developed LTCC-based biosensor showed a linear response to DA concentrations from 1μM up to 200μM, which was similar to the results reported by Demirkol et al. [38] and Roy et al. [39]. The corresponding dynamic responses to different concentrations of the substrate are depicted in Fig. 15 .

The coefficient of the determination for the linear relationship of these parameters is close to 1 (R2 =0.988), which indicates the accuracy of the fabricated sensor in a given concentration range for the substrate (1–200μM). As reported in literature, the concentration of DA in human urine is nearly 2.6μM [40]. For this reason, the obtained linear range for the determination of this catecholamine fits medical purposes.

The sensor was tested to determine the influence of its operating condition flow rate on its response and the highest value of fluorescence intensity. For this purpose, sensor responses to various concentrations of the substrate and to different values of the flow rate (100–500μl/min) were measured. The LTCC-based biosensor output signals for these different flow rates are presented in Fig. 16 . As demonstrated in Fig. 16, the best flow rate was 300μl/min: the quickest response of the sensor, and the highest value of fluorescence intensity, so this value was chosen for the further experiments.

Medium value flow rates rendered better results,which can be explained by the fact that the enzyme-substrate contact is longer, leading to a generation of a greater amount of reaction product. This, in turn, increases the signal and the accuracy of the measurements is higher.

A variety of specimens in natural fluids can interfere with the significance of quantitative determination of dopamine. All interference specimens were added in excess (200%) in relation to the concentration of dopamine. Subsequently, the selectivity of the measuring procedure was investigated. Fig. 17 shows that the widespread substances, including ascorbic acid (AA), uric acid (UA), cysteine (CYS), glutathione (GLU) and a mix of all tested reagents had nearly no effect (or the effect of ≤3%, indicating negligible interference) on fluorescence intensity of the samples compared with the blank. Ascorbic acid as well as uric acid did not affect the selectivity of the fabricated setup, and the existing interference did not affect the accuracy of the proposed dopamine test. The use of laccase in the detection system enabled a successful detection of lower DA concentrations (>10μM) and the interfering compounds had no significant impact on the measurements. Therefore, the biosensor exhibited a satisfactory selectivity for DA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility and stability of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The limit of detection (LOD) for dopamine was found at 80nM based on S/D=3. Moreover, the relative standard deviation (RSD) was characterized by 6.2%, 4.7%, and 5.5% for three independent measurements of 1, 10, and 40μM of dopamine, respectively. The result can point to convenience and reliability of the proposed sensing procedure.

To evaluate the feasibility of the presented procedure, the fluorescence assay was applied to determine the dopamine level in a labeled pharmacological product (Dopaminum Hydrochloricum, 40mg/ml (concentration=0.27μM), Polfa Warszawa). The analytical results for the labeled samples are given in Table 1 . To perform a real sample analysis, it was necessary to add 20μM of DA to the pharmacological product (the drug itself has a very low concentration of DA − beyond the linear range and detection limit of our setup). The good recovery value (calculated as a ratio of the detected concentration to the real concentration of dopamine in the sample (%) demonstrated clearly the accuracy of the presented fluorescence method for a useful DA detection. It is also possible to analyze real biological samples (e.g. urine, serum) in the presented fluorescence-based system in the same way as pharmaceutical samples were analyzed for determination of DA. A human sample (e.g. urine) should be mixed (without any further preparation) with standard DA and GQDs solution, and then measurements should be executed using a constructed setup, in the way similar to the method reported in literature [9,41]. Methods for DA detection in real samples do not require any special approach in respect of selective and sensitive biosensors. In both cases urine or serum samples were spiked with standard DA solution and tested.

The study demonstrated a method for fabrication of the flow-through fluorescence laccase-based biosensor for DA detection. The device was made using the LTCC technology. Screen-printed Pt electrode was suitable for modification with poly(dithienotetraphenylsilane) which was an adequate matrix for the immobilization of laccase. The enzyme was attached to the substrate and preserved its high catalytic activity. Fluorescence measurements indicated that the designed setup operated with high accuracy and reliability. Accordingly, the detection limit for dopamine was established at 80nM. No interference from ascorbic acid as well as uric acid was found. The main advantages of the presented sensor are: sensitivity, precision, linearity and simplicity of construction. This type of designed setup makes continuous analysis possible.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge Wrocław University of Science and Technology and National Science Centre (grant no. 2016/23/B/ST7/00932) for the financial support. The authors are also grateful to PhD Eng. Adam Szyszka from the Faculty of Microsystem Electronics and Photonics, Wrocław University of Science and Technology for his help in the preparation of AFM images and calculations.

Science

Sci. Rep.

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Joanna Cabaj, Ph.D., D.Sc. has received her Ph.D. in organic chemistry in Wroclaw University of Science and Technology, Poland, in 2005. After habilitation (2012, biotechnology) she works as an assistant professor at Faculty of Chemistry in Wroclaw University of Science and Technology. Her research interests include developing of novel semiconducting materials for molecular electronics and sensoric devices. After PhD, her researches have been connected with electroconducting properties of well-ordered thin Langmuir–Blodgett and Langmuir-Schaefer films built of symmetric heterocyclic amphiphiles for construction of simple gas sensors. One of the most important research interests of Joanna Cabaj include also fabrication of small molecular biosensors (electrochemical, optical) based on enzymes immobilized in thin ordered layers obtained from synthesized macro-heterocyclic compounds as well as different types of nanoparticles. She is co-author of 55 publications, 8 book chapters (international publishing houses) as well as 30 patents and several patent declarations. She was awarded by the Rector of Wroclaw University of Science and Technology award for patent works in 2006 and 2007 as well as she was granted as young scientist.

Sylwia Baluta (M.Sc.) was graduated in 2014 in the field of Biotechnology and Medicinal Chemistry (2015) at Wrocław University of Science and Technology. Since 2015 she is a PhD student at Wrocław University of Science and Technology, Faculty of Chemistry. Her scientific interests include enzyme-based sensors for neurotransmitters determination, based on electrochemical and optical measurements.

Karol Malecha was born in Poland in 1981. He received his MSc degree in Material Science Engineering from Wroclaw University of Science and Technology, Poland in 2005. In 2009 at the same University he received a PhD degree in electronics by public defense of his dissertation entitled “Microfluidic systems made with LTCC technology”. In 2014, he received his Doctor of Science degree in electronics. Currently he is employed at the Faculty of Electronics Microsystem and Photonics, Wrocław University of Science and Technology as an assistant professor, where he is involved in modeling and manufacturing of microfluidic systems made with LTCC technology. He is also a member of the IMAPS Poland Chapter.

Professor Jadwiga Soloducho is an organic chemist, her interests concern with synthesis and physicochemical properties of macromolecular heterocycle structures. During 2002-2005 she was a head of Institute of Organic Chemistry, Biochemistry and Biotechnology. During 2008-2012 she was a v-ce Dean of Faculty of Chemistry of Wroclaw University of Technology. Since October 2012 to September 2015 she was a head of Department of Medicinal Chemistry and Microbiology of Wroclaw University of Technology. In recent years, Professor Sołoducho’s researches concern to competitive materials for electronic and optoelectronic technologies. In her investigations is synthesis of a new organic monomers based on ethylenedioxythiophene, thiophene, thiazole, furane, thianthrene, xanthene, phenothiazine, phenoxazine, tetraphenylsilane, anthracene. In field of her interests is also adequate correlations between electric intensity and toxic gases concentration. Moreover, she is interested in the results of research directed into the properties of protein hybrid layers used as biosensors. Professor J. Soloducho was a few times awarded by Rectors of Wroclaw University of Technology (in example Docendo Discimus-2012, gold award of Wrocław University of Technology-2013), and by Polish Science and Education Ministry (Maria Curie Sklodowska Award- the brown medal, 2011). Since 1997 she is also co-organizer of Lower Silesia Science Festival. She is an author of more than one hundred twenty original publications, ten book chapters in international publishing houses, more than 60 patents and several patent declarations.

Dorota Zając received her Ph.D. from the Wroclaw University of Science and Technology in 2017, under the supervision of Prof. Jadwiga Sołoducho. Her current research involves the design and synthesis of heterocyclic compounds for achieving both high electron transfer efficiency and the widest range of absorbance. The designed compounds are potentially materials to build a sensor device or organic solar cells.
